FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, U.S. sample, N=7514, ages 9-10 at baseline 2016-2018

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time (self-reported hours per day, weekday/weekend); problematic social media use via SMAQ scale. Qualifying social media variable. B1 applies (separate SM coefficient reported).

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression with mediation analysis via GSEM; indirect effects via 5000 bootstrap samples; mediation test p=0.002; Table 2, Table 3

FT_IC4.evidence: Risk ratios with 95% CI: SM time RR 1.31 (1.20-1.43) p<0.001 (Table 2, Model 1); indirect effect B=0.011 with BC 95% CI (0.007, 0.016); proportion mediated 25.0% (11.0, 39.3) p=0.001 (Table 3)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study prospective data; prospective association tested Year 1 → Year 3 with mediator at Year 2

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplication; published 2024 in Substance Use & Misuse

FT_EC7_reason: SM is not the sole dependent variable. Paper tests SM→outcome (alcohol sipping), not SM as dependent variable only. Condition (a) fails: paper has SM→non-SM-outcome analysis.
notes: All six inclusion criteria clearly MET. Prospective 3-year design with mediation pathway. Mixed-effects Poisson regression with proper covariate adjustment.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, U.S. sample, N=4,718 adolescents aged 10-15, 2018-2021

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure is screen time (recreational hours per day) but no qualifying smartphone or social media variable. Screen time alone without device/platform specification fails B5 and B7 criteria. No SM platform, no app-specific measure, no SM time reported separately.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression; multivariate models testing associations between screen time categories and CVD risk factors with 95% CI; Table 2 reports coefficients

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients with 95% CI: high screen time diastolic BP 4.27 (1.83-6.73) percentile, HDL B=-2.85 (95% CI -4.77 to -0.94) mg/dL (Table 2)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis; cross-sectional design examining screen time and CVD biomarkers

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplication evident; published 2024 in BMC Public Health

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (not SM) is independent variable. FT_EC7 does not apply to non-SM exposures.
notes: FAILS FT_IC2: unspecified screen time without SM or smartphone variables. Paper studies TV, movies, videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media as categories but does not report separate coefficients for each screen type in primary analyses. Lacks qualifying SM/smartphone measure as primary exposure.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, U.S. sample, N=9,538 adolescents aged 9-10 at baseline 2016-2018 with 2-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey; self-reported hours per day including specific types: television, videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media. Specific screen types reported with separate associations (video chat, texting, videos, video games). B1 applies: separate β coefficients reported for screen types.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects linear regression with random effects; t-scores reported for associations between baseline screen time and CBCL mental health scores at follow-up; Table 2 and stratified analyses

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients with 95% CI: total screen time vs depressive symptoms B=0.10 (0.06, 0.13) p<0.001; conduct B=0.07 (0.03, 0.10) p<0.001; somatic B=0.06 (0.01, 0.11) p=0.026; ADHD B=0.06 (0.01, 0.10) p=0.013 (Table 2)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD prospective cohort analysis; baseline screen time (Year 0/1) predicts CBCL outcomes at Year 2 follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplication; published 2024 in BMC Public Health

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is IV predicting mental health outcomes (CBCL scores), not SM as DV. Paper tests screen time→outcome, not demographics→SM. Condition (a) fails.
notes: All six inclusion criteria clearly MET. Prospective 2-year design. Mixed-effects models with proper covariate adjustment for age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, data collection period, study site. Stratified analyses by sex and race/ethnicity reported.
